cards.text: To define a class template: {{c1::template}} <{{c2::typename T}}> struct Pair { T first; T second; };

cards.back: A blueprint for creating classes where one or more types are parameterized (specified when instantiating the template).

cards.question: What does this create?
cards.answer: Creates a Pair object with int type members (first=5, second=10).
cards.explanation: Template parameter T is replaced with int.

cards.text: To instantiate a class template: Pair{{c1::<int>}} p { 5, 10 }; (specify {{c2::template argument}} in angle brackets)

cards.front: What is the difference between a template parameter and template argument?
cards.back:
Template parameter: Placeholder in definition (e.g., T)
Template argument: Actual type when instantiating (e.g., int)

cards.question: Can you create multiple instantiations?
cards.answer: Yes. Each instantiation creates a different type (Box<int>, Box<double>, Box<std::string>).
cards.explanation: Templates generate code for each type argument used.

cards.front: Can a class template have multiple template parameters?
cards.back: Yes. Example: template <typename T, typename U> struct Pair { T first; U second; };

cards.text: Both {{c1::typename}} and {{c2::class}} keywords can be used for template type parameters (they're equivalent).

cards.question: What types are T and U here?
cards.explanation: Template arguments are matched to parameters in order.

Class templates provide:
  • Type safety without code duplication
  • Compile-time specialization
  • Works with any type
  • Zero runtime overhead
Used extensively in STL (vector, map, etc.)
examples: template <typename T> struct Box { T val; };

cards.front: When is a class template actually compiled?
cards.back: When it's instantiated with specific template arguments. The template itself is not compiled until used.

cards.text: Class templates use {{c1::template parameters}} (like T) that are replaced with {{c2::template arguments}} (like int) during instantiation.
Both {{c1::typename}} and {{c2::class}} keywords can introduce template type parameters and are equivalent.
Templates are compiled when {{c1::instantiated}} with specific template arguments, not when {{c2::defined}}.
Class templates use {{c1::template parameters}} (like T) that are replaced with {{c2::template arguments}} (like int) during instantiation.
Both {{c1::typename}} and {{c2::class}} keywords can introduce template type parameters and are equivalent.
